title: Anansi Boys
summary: Anansi's sons—Fat Face and Small Head—continue their fathers trickery in the Caribbean.
full_text:
In the vibrant, sun-soaked markets and colorful streets of Jamaica, the air is always filled with music, laughter, and the art of the "trick." This spirit of cleverness is the legacy of Anansi, the great spider-god of West Africa who traveled across the ocean in the hearts of the enslaved, becoming a symbol of survival and resistance in the Caribbean.

But Anansi did not leave behind just stories; he left behind his sons—Fat Face and Small Head. Though they lacked their father's eight legs, they inherited his eight-fold cunning. Fat Face and Small Head were not interested in the grand designs of gods; they were interested in the immediate pleasure of a full belly and a small fortune.

One afternoon, the brothers arrived at the gates of a wealthy plantation owner's estate. The man was known for his greed and his disdain for the poor, but he had a weakness for the exotic and the miraculous. "Sir!" Small Head cried, his voice a symphony of faux-excitement. "We are the world's greatest circus performers! We possess magic that would make the stars dance! Pay us a handful of gold, and we shall show you a miracle."

Intrigued, the rich man agreed. He led them to a courtyard and waited. Fat Face sat upon a simple wooden bucket, looking solemn and focused. Small Head stepped forward and, with a flourish of his hands and a loud "Hocus Pocus!", flipped the bucket over. "Behold!" he shouted. "Now there are TWO buckets!"

The rich man blinked. He looked at the two buckets—one on top of the other—and for a moment, his greed clouded his judgment. He was so eager to be fooled that he actually believed he had witnessed a duplication of matter. He cheered and tossed them a gold coin.

But the brothers were not done. Small Head stood on his head, balancing perfectly. "And now, I shall walk upon the air with my feet in the clouds!" he claimed. As he performed a clumsy flip, his trousers fell down, revealing a pair of brightly colored undergarments. The crowd burst into laughter, and the rich man, finally realizing he had been played, chased them from his land with a cane.

The brothers ran, laughing all the way, their gold coins jingling in their pockets. They eventually sought refuge with a local priest, confessing their trickery with a wink. The priest, amused by their audacity, forgave them, noting that they had simply applied the "Anansi Method" to a man who deserved to be humbled.

The stories of the Anansi Boys are more than just comedies; they are lessons in survival. In a world where the powerful often hold all the cards, wit is the only weapon available to the marginalized. Fat Face and Small Head represent the enduring human spirit—the ability to find a loophole in the system, to laugh in the face of oppression, and to turn a simple bucket into a source of freedom. They remind us that while strength can build a wall, only cleverness can find the way over it.
moral: Wit survives all—use your brains.
source: Jamaican Folklore (Anansi tradition)

title: How Anansi Got His Stories
summary: Long ago, all stories in the forest belonged to Tiger, and were called Tiger Stories. Anansi, tired of being mocked as the weakest creature, demanded that stories be named after him. Tiger set an impossible task: capture Snake. After failing with a noose, a pit trap, and a cage, Anansi tricked Snake by challenging his length against bamboo. Anansi tied Snake's head and tail to the bamboo, proving that brains can overcome brawn. From that day, all stories became Anansi Stories. The phrase "Jack Mandora, mi nuh choose none" is traditionally said at the end to absolve the storyteller of Anansi's mischievous deeds.
source: Martha Warren Beckwith, Jamaica Anansi Stories (1924); Real Jamaica Vacations
moral: Brains can overcome brawn

title: The Rolling Calf
summary: One of Jamaica's most feared duppies, the Rolling Calf appears as a massive jet-black bull with blazing red eyes that gash fire, dragging heavy chains that clank and spark as it moves. It haunts lonely roads, bridle paths through cane fields, and crossroads at night. The Rolling Calf is believed to be the restless spirit of a wicked person — often a cruel butcher or a brutal plantation overseer from slavery days. To escape, one must lie flat in a ditch off the road, scratch a cross in the earth, throw a garment as a decoy, or carry protective herbs like vervain. The legend transforms the chains of slavery into a supernatural symbol of guilt that can be outwitted.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; Fiwi Roots; Real Jamaica Vacations; Laura Tanna fieldwork
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

title: Ol' Hige the Vampiric Witch
summary: Ol' Hige is a witch from Jamaican folklore who sheds her skin at night and flies through the air in the form of an owl or a ball of fire. She harms people — especially babies — by sucking their breath while they sleep. Her habit of shedding her skin makes her vulnerable: if someone discovers her empty skin and fills it with salt and pepper, she will be unable to put it back on, burning terribly, and can be killed at dawn. Babies are traditionally protected from Ol' Hige by placing an open Bible in their cribs or using special charms. The figure is of West African origin and is known in Trinidad as the Soucouyant.
source: Real Jamaica Vacations; Jamaican oral tradition
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: River Mumma and the Golden Table
summary: River Mumma is a female water spirit who guards the sources of Jamaica's rivers. She is sometimes seen sitting by the river, combing her long hair with a golden comb, leaving the comb on rocks to entice victims whom she then draws into the water. Associated with her is the Legend of the Golden Table: a table of pure gold lies at the bottom of certain rivers, rising briefly to the surface at midday. Whoever sees it becomes mesmerized and obsessed with obtaining it, but efforts to procure the table always end in disaster as the river claims their lives. The legend likely arose from the Spanish quest for gold in Jamaica. In slavery times, sacrifices were offered to River Mumma in drought.
source: Real Jamaica Vacations; Jamaican oral tradition
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Duppy: The Restless Spirits of Jamaica
summary: Duppies are restless spirits in Jamaican folklore, believed to be the souls of the dead who have not found peace. Good duppies appear in dreams (called 'dream you') to give advice or information from deceased family and friends. Bad duppies can cause harm and may be set on someone through obeah (sorcery). Duppies are said to speak in high-pitched nasal voices. Signs of a duppy's presence include a feeling that one's head is growing or unexplained heat. Protection methods include eating salt (which duppies despise), wearing clothes inside out, cursing, or exposing oneself to shame the spirit. Specific duppies include the Three-Footed Horse and Rolling Calf.
source: Wikipedia; Real Jamaica Vacations; Jamaican oral tradition
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

title: Anansi and Tiger in the Pit
summary: Anansi hears Tiger roaring and finds him trapped in a deep pit. As Tiger claws his way up inch by inch, Anansi taunts him from above. When Tiger reaches near the top, gasping that he has no more strength, Anansi mockingly advises him to pray — to put his paws together and pray for strength. The moment Tiger puts his paws together, he slides all the way back down into the pit while Anansi runs off laughing. This classic Anansi trickster tale demonstrates how the spider uses cunning and psychological manipulation to defeat a much stronger opponent, a theme that resonated deeply with enslaved Africans in Jamaica.
source: Tiny Tales of Anansi by Laura Gibbs; Pressbooks
moral: Brains can overcome brawn

title: Papa Legba and the Gate of the Spirit World
summary: Papa Legba is the most important lwa (spirit) in Haitian Vodou, serving as the gatekeeper between the human and spirit worlds. No human can communicate with any other spirit without Papa Legba's permission. He stands at the spiritual crossroads, deciding whether a person is allowed to speak with other lwa. Believed to speak all human languages, Papa Legba is known as the great elocutioner. In Vodou ceremonies, he is always the first and last spirit invoked, as he opens and closes the gateway. His origins trace to the West African kingdom of Dahomey (modern Benin), where he was known as Legba, the trickster messenger god of the Fon and Yoruba peoples.
source: AllThatsInteresting.com; Study.com; Lwa and Order
moral: Brains can overcome brawn

title: Baron Samedi, Lord of the Dead
summary: Baron Samedi (Baron Saturday) is one of the most iconic lwa of Haitian Vodou, the master of death, tombs, graveyards, and resurrection. He is depicted wearing a top hat, black tail coat, dark glasses, and cotton plugs in the nostrils like a corpse prepared for burial. Often shown as a skeleton, he speaks in a nasal voice and is known for disruption, debauchery, and his fondness for tobacco and rum. As head of the Gede family of lwa, he has the final say on death itself and is also a healer and protector of souls. His consort is Maman Brigitte. Baron Samedi can grant resurrection as well as death, making him a figure of both fear and respect.
source: Wikipedia; StorytellingDB.com; HoodooQ
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

title: Ti Malice and Bouki: The Trickster and the Fool
summary: Ti Malice and Uncle Bouki are paired characters in Haitian folklore, representing the split of the Anansi trickster archetype. Ti Malice is smart, cunning, and guileful, while Uncle Bouki is hardworking but greedy and foolish. In one classic tale, Bouki hears an old woman cry 'Ay-yai!' after biting her lip, and mistakes it for the name of a delicious food. He rushes to the market asking for 'ay-yai' and is laughed at. Ti Malice then fills a bag with random items and sells it to Bouki as 'ay-yai,' teaching him a lesson. The characters originated in West African oral traditions where Bouki is a hyena (from Fulani/Wolof 'bouki') and Malice is a hare called Leuk in Senegal.
source: Wikipedia; The Cultured Chef; TeacherPayTeachers
moral: Brains can overcome brawn

title: The Zombie Creation Myth of Haitian Vodou
summary: The zombie (zonbi) originates from Haitian folklore, where it is a dead body reanimated through magic by a bokor sorcerer. In Vodou belief, a person has two souls: the ti bon ange (little good angel) and the gros bon ange (big good angel). The bokor captures the ti bon ange, the essence of personality and consciousness, bottling it while the body becomes a soulless slave. The Bizango secret societies are said to have used zombification as social control, poisoning individuals who transgressed codes and then providing antidotes to leave them in a pliant, obedient state. The zombie metaphor reflects the terror of enslavement — being alive but without will or identity. The word comes from Kikongo 'nzumbi' meaning spirit of a dead person.
source: Wikipedia; Overthink podcast; Purdue e-Pubs; Temple University Press
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: Changó, the Lord of Thunder and Fire
summary: Changó (Shangó) is the powerful orisha of thunder, lightning, fire, and war in Cuban Santería/Lucumí, syncretized with Saint Barbara in Catholic tradition. According to myth, Changó was once a king of the Yoruba city of Oyo who was deified after death. He wields a double-headed axe and dances with fiery intensity. Changó's legends tell of his battles, his many lovers including Oshún and Oyá, and his terrible temper. The batá drums are sacred to him, and his number is 6. A famous patakí (sacred story) tells how Changó received his powers from Obatalá and how he used thunder to defeat his enemies. In Cuba, Changó became a symbol of resistance and masculine power for enslaved Africans.
source: Wikipedia; GoddessGift.com; Aroma de Cuba
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Yemayá, Mother of the Ocean
summary: Yemayá (Yemoja) is the great mother orisha of the ocean, maternity, and the moon in Cuban Santería, syncretized with the Virgin of Regla. She is the goddess of living waters, the salt sea, and is considered the mother of all life and the protector of women and children. Her colors are blue and white, her number is 7, and her symbol is a fan. A famous patakí tells how Yemayá gave her long magical hair to her sister Oshún as a gift of generosity, binding their realms of salt and fresh water forever. Yemayá is depicted as a mermaid or a beautiful woman emerging from the waves. She represents the maternal, protective, and sometimes fierce aspect of the divine feminine. Her domain is the ocean's depth and mystery.
source: Wikipedia; Center for Folklore Studies (Ohio State); Mythlok
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

title: Oshún, Goddess of the River
summary: Oshún (Ochún) is the orisha of fresh water, love, fertility, beauty, and sensuality in Cuban Santería, syncretized with the Virgen de la Caridad del Cobre, Cuba's patron saint. According to the Ifá literary corpus, Oshún was the only female irunmole (primordial spirit) sent to assist in creating the world. The male spirits initially ignored her, and the world failed. Only when her feminine wisdom and sweetness were incorporated did creation flourish. Her colors are gold, yellow, and coral, her number is 5, and her symbols include honey, mirrors, fans, and peacocks. She is represented as a beautiful mulata in Cuban tradition. Oshún is both a healer and a seductress, teaching that sweetness and joy are essential forces of creation alongside power and strength.
source: Wikipedia; Mythologis; GoddessGift.com
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: Eleggua, the Trickster Messenger
summary: Eleggua (Elegbara) is the trickster orisha of crossroads, doors, opportunities, and fate in Cuban Santería. He is the messenger between humanity and the other orishas, syncretized with the Holy Child of Atocha. Eleggua opens and closes paths, and no ceremony can begin without his permission. He is depicted as a mischievous child who carries a hooked staff and wears a red and black beaded necklace. His number is 3, multiplied by 21. One legend tells how Eleggua created confusion among the orishas by wearing a hat of two colors, causing arguments about which way he was walking — teaching that perspective and truth are often deceptive. Eleggua is considered the first orisha that initiates receive, making him fundamental to all of Santería practice.
source: GoddessGift.com; Aroma de Cuba; Anywhere.com Cuba
moral: Brains can overcome brawn

title: The Legend of the Coquí
summary: In Puerto Rican folklore, the tiny coquí frog is beloved for its distinctive two-note call that fills the island's nights with song. One Taino legend tells that the coquí was once a vain and boastful frog who challenged the sun to a singing contest. The sun burned the frog so badly that he shrunk to a tiny size and was forced to hide in the shade by day, emerging only at night to sing. Another version speaks of the goddess Atabey creating the coquí as a symbol of resilience for the Puerto Rican people — the frog's song is a sacred balm that fills every Puerto Rican heart, reminding them that no matter how many times they fall, they can always rise again, just as the coquí sings on after every storm.
source: Magic in Puerto Rico; Vamos Rincón; What's Up Rincón
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: La Garita: The Goat Children
summary: La Garita (also known as the Goat Children or the Legend of the Garita) is a Puerto Rican folktale about two children who were transformed into goats as punishment or protection. The story is traditionally told to explain the presence of wild goats in the Puerto Rican countryside and to warn children against disobedience. The legend varies across the island, with some versions telling of children who mocked a holy figure and were turned into goats as a curse, while others speak of children who fled into the mountains and were transformed into animals by protective spirits. The garita itself — a sentry box or small structure — became associated with these mysterious goat-children who are said to still roam the island's remote hills, their bleating heard on quiet nights.
source: Puerto Rican oral tradition; What's Up Rincón
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

title: Juan Bobo: The Folk Simpleton
summary: Juan Bobo (Simple John) is a beloved folk character in Puerto Rican and broader Caribbean folklore — a good-hearted but dim-witted boy whose adventures always go hilariously wrong. In one classic tale, Juan Bobo's mother asks him to watch the pot of rice while she goes to church. He decides to bathe the pig with the rice water, then feeds the rice to the chickens, explaining that he was keeping everything clean. In another, when told to mind the baby, he dresses the family dog in the baby's clothes. Despite his foolishness, Juan Bobo stories carry gentle moral lessons about honesty, kindness, and the importance of listening carefully to instructions. He represents the everyman who, through innocence rather than malice, reveals life's absurdities.
source: Puerto Rican oral tradition; StoryMaps ArcGIS
moral: Kindness and innocence reveal life's absurdities

title: La Ciguapa of the Dominican Mountains
summary: La Ciguapa is a legendary creature from Dominican folklore — a beautiful woman with long, flowing hair and dark skin who has her feet on backwards. She inhabits the remote mountainous regions of the Dominican Republic, particularly the Cordillera Central and Bahoruco Mountains. According to legend, La Ciguapa lures men into the wilderness with her stunning beauty, only to leave them hopelessly lost, confused, or dead. Her backwards footprints make it impossible to track her, as they appear to lead in the opposite direction. Some say the legend may have Taino origins, while others see influences from European mermaid myths. The Ciguapa embodies the danger and mystery of the Dominican wilderness, serving as a warning against wandering alone in remote areas and the perils of unchecked desire.
source: Wikipedia; Island Hopper Guides; The Horror Collection
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: The Legend of Enriquillo and Anacaona
summary: The legend of Enriquillo and Anacaona is one of the most powerful stories of Taino resistance in Hispaniola (modern Dominican Republic and Haiti). Anacaona (Golden Flower) was a Taino queen and cacica (female chief) known for her poetry, music, and diplomacy. She ruled the province of Jaragua and initially sought peaceful coexistence with the Spanish. Despite her efforts, she was captured and executed by hanging in 1503 on false charges of conspiracy. Enriquillo, her nephew, led the most successful Taino rebellion in history from 1519 to 1533, using guerrilla tactics from the Bahoruco Mountains. He eventually secured a peace treaty that granted him and his followers land and autonomy. Both figures symbolize indigenous resistance against colonial oppression and remain cultural icons in the Dominican Republic.
source: Dominican oral tradition; Island Hopper Guides
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Papa Bois, the Guardian of the Trinidadian Forest
summary: Papa Bois (Father of the Woods) is the ancient protector spirit of Trinidad's forests, a figure of Afro-Trinidadian Creole folklore with roots in West African forest guardians. He appears as an impossibly old man with bark-like skin, a mossy beard tangled with vines, and deer-like legs ending in cloven hooves, with stag antlers crowning his head. He speaks the languages of all animals and blows a conch shell horn to warn poachers they are seen. For those who hunt with respect and take only what they need, Papa Bois guides them to game. For greedy hunters and wasteful destroyers, he makes forest paths vanish, moves landmarks, and traps them in an endless green labyrinth. He embodies the sacred balance between humans and nature.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; Wikipedia; C.R. Ottley, Folklore of Trinidad
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

title: La Diablesse, the Devil Woman
summary: La Diablesse is a seductive she-devil from Trinidadian folklore who appears as a breathtakingly beautiful woman in an old-fashioned embroidered dress and wide-brimmed hat, smelling of expensive perfume. By moonlight, the perceptive may glimpse her true nature — one cloven hoof hidden beneath the hem. She lures unfaithful husbands, drunken braggarts, and morally weak men by catching their gaze and walking away, her petticoat making a rhythmic chac-chac sound. She leads them deep into the wilderness until she vanishes at the edge of a ravine, leaving them lost, injured, or dead. La Diablesse does not physically attack; she delivers men to their own punishment. On full moon nights, wise villagers keep close to home and company.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; NALIS; C.R. Ottley
moral: Respect for nature and the spirits brings safety and fortune

title: The Soucouyant, the Vampire of Trinidad
summary: The Soucouyant is one of the most feared beings in Trinidadian folklore — a vampire-like creature who appears by day as a harmless old woman living alone in a shack with shuttered windows. At midnight, she sheds her human skin, hiding it in a wooden mortar, and transforms into a searing ball of fire that slips through keyholes and cracks to hunt. She targets sleeping infants and children, slowly draining their blood and leaving them pale and weak with tiny pinprick marks. To repel her, scatter uncooked rice or salt at the threshold — she is compelled to stop and count every grain until dawn. To destroy her permanently, find her hidden skin during the day and fill it with salt or pepper; when she tries to reclothe herself at dawn, the burning will destroy her spirit in the sunlight.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; C.R. Ottley; J.D. Elder
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: The Douen: Lost Children of Trinidad
summary: Douen are the spirits of unbaptized children who died before receiving Christian names in Trinidadian folklore. They appear as small children wearing large straw hats that hide their faces, and they walk with their feet pointing backwards — making their footprints appear to go in the opposite direction. Douen are known to call out the names of living children, luring them into the forest to become lost. Parents warn children never to respond to a voice calling their name in the woods unless they can see the person. Once lured by a Douen, a child may wander into the wilderness and never return, becoming a Douen themselves. The legend serves as both a cautionary tale about wandering alone and a folk explanation for missing children in rural Trinidad.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; Trinidadian oral tradition
moral: Respect for nature and the spirits brings safety and fortune

title: The Chickcharney of Andros Island
summary: The Chickcharney is a legendary creature from Bahamian folklore, specifically from Andros Island. It resembles a small, elf-like being with owl-like feathers, glowing red eyes, three fingers on each hand, and three toes on each foot. It walks upright and is said to whistle softly as it moves. According to tradition, how a person treats the Chickcharney determines whether luck or misfortune follows. Construction workers and lumberjacks on Andros Island would leave small offerings of food at the edge of their work sites as gestures of respect. Those who showed respect had safe workdays and found missing tools returned neatly nearby. Those who mocked the old warnings suffered accidents, illness, and misfortune. The Chickcharney embodies the principle that the forests hold memory, presence, and power deserving of respect.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; Wikipedia; Discover Bahamas; Daniel J. Crowley, Folk-Tales of Andros Island, Bahamas
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

title: Atabey, Mother of Waters and Creation
summary: Atabey (also Atabex) is the supreme mother goddess of the Taino people, the indigenous inhabitants of the Greater Antilles. She is the goddess of fresh water, fertility, the earth, and the moon. According to Taino creation myth, Atabey gave birth to herself from all the elements before creating the world. She then produced twin sons: Yocahú (also called Yucaju), the god of cassava and the sea, and Guacar, who became Juracán, the god of storms and chaos. After creating the world, Atabey found it empty and lonely, so she and Yocahú filled it with living beings, plants, and natural wonders. Guacar grew jealous of their bond and became Juracán, conjuring hurricanes to dismantle their creations. Atabey is depicted in a birthing position in petroglyphs found at Caguana, Puerto Rico, and Taino women called upon her during childbirth.
source: Order of the White Moon; A.P. Mobley; Wikipedia
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: The Cave of Caguana: Taino Creation
summary: According to Taino mythology, all human life began in a sacred cave called Cacibajagua (Cave of the Jagua) on the island of Haiti (Hispaniola). The first humans emerged from this cave, while the gods dwelt in the Cave of the Serpent and the Cave of Coaybay. The deity Iguanaboína was among the first beings from the river. The Taino believed their ancestors originated from this cave system, with different groups emerging at different times. Those who emerged late were said to have been turned into stone by the morning sun — explaining certain rock formations. The cave of Caguana in Puerto Rico contains some of the most important Taino petroglyphs, depicting Atabey in her birthing position, marking it as a sacred ceremonial center for areítos (ceremonial dances and songs).
source: Wikipedia; West Side Destination; Smithsonian
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: Juracán, Lord of the Storm
summary: Juracán is the Taino god of storms and hurricanes, the origin of the English word 'hurricane.' In Taino mythology, Juracán was once Guacar, the twin brother of Yocahú, son of the mother goddess Atabey. While Atabey and Yocahú worked together filling the world with beautiful creations, Guacar grew jealous of their bond. His bitterness transformed him into Juracán, a destructive zemi of chaos who, alongside his wife Guabancex (goddess of storms), conjures devastating hurricanes to dismantle his mother's and brother's creations. The Taino believed Juracán dwelt in the mountains and unleashed storms when angered. The destructive power of Caribbean hurricanes was thus explained as divine conflict between creation and chaos. Cemí figures representing Juracán were carved with twisted, furious faces.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; West Side Destination; A.P. Mobley
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: The First Areíto Drum
summary: A Taíno legend explaining the origin of the sacred areíto drum. Before the drum, the Taíno remembered everything through spoken words alone, but as elders passed on and storms scattered villages, memories faltered and disputes arose over boundaries and promises. One night, a cacique (chief) prayed to the zemís asking how memory could be protected. From the forest emerged an old man carrying a hollowed log who taught the people to stretch animal hide across one end, creating the first drum. The drum would carry law, lineage, and story — guiding singers so words would not wander. Laws were sung with the drum's beat, agreements sealed through areíto performances, and the drum was never struck in anger. It belonged to the community, not any individual, and kept Taíno memory alive through rhythm.
source: FolktalesAmerica.com; Smithsonian Folkways archives
moral: The world is shaped by powerful forces both creative and destructive

title: Mama Dlo, Mother of the Waters
summary: Mama Dlo (also known as Mama D'Leau or Mama Glo) is a water spirit from Trinidadian folklore, the wife of Papa Bois. She is the mother of the waters, a beautiful woman from the waist up but a serpent or fish from the waist down — similar to a mermaid. Mama Dlo guards rivers, ponds, and wetlands, and is known to grant riches and favors to those who treat the waters with respect. She is notoriously protective of her domain and exacts revenge on those who pollute or overfish. Like many Caribbean water spirits, she combs her long hair by the water's edge, and anyone who captures her golden comb can demand a wish. However, dealing with Mama Dlo is dangerous — she may drown those who offend her or lure children too close to the water's edge. Her name comes from French Creole 'Maman de l'Eau' (Mother of the Water).
source: Life in Trinidad and Tobago; Caribbean Folklore with Illustration
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe
